title: RespiroSync Protocol: A Phase-Locked Loop Framework for Robust Respiratory Rate Estimation via Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion
author: Davis, Felix D.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This manuscript introduces the RespiroSync Protocol (RSP), a novel phase-locked loop (PLL) framework for robust respiratory rate estimation (breaths per minute, BPM) using multi-modal sensor fusion. The protocol integrates signals from accelerometry, photoplethysmography, and chest impedance, employing Hilbert transform-based phase extraction (ϕ(t) = arg(H[s(t)])) and power spectral density analysis for motion artifact rejection. The PLL optimizes the objective J(θ) = E[∥ϕref(t) − ϕest(t; θ)∥²₂] + λ∫|H(ω)|²dω, with adaptive gain parameters (Kp, Ki) ensuring stability. Comorbidity scaling factors (γc) adjust BPM estimates for conditions like COPD and asthma, enhancing clinical relevance. Validation on 1,800 subjects demonstrates a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.3 ± 0.4 BPM, root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.7 ± 0.5 BPM, and 94.2% accuracy within ±2 BPM, with 89.7% reliability in high-motion scenarios. Compared to baseline methods (e.g., FFT, Kalman filter), RSP achieves superior performance (p < 0.05). 
 Future work includes real-world trials in rural African clinics and machine learning for automatic comorbidity detection, targeting a 10% MAE reduction. This framework is poised for clinical and consumer health applications, particularly in continuous monitoring for telemedicine.
steps_raw:
This manuscript introduces the RespiroSync Protocol (RSP), a novel phase-locked loop (PLL) framework for robust respiratory rate estimation (breaths per minute, BPM) using multi-modal sensor fusion. The protocol integrates signals from accelerometry, photoplethysmography, and chest impedance, employing Hilbert transform-based phase extraction (ϕ(t) = arg(H[s(t)])) and power spectral density analysis for motion artifact rejection. The PLL optimizes the objective J(θ) = E[∥ϕref(t) − ϕest(t; θ)∥²₂] + λ∫|H(ω)|²dω, with adaptive gain parameters (Kp, Ki) ensuring stability. Comorbidity scaling factors (γc) adjust BPM estimates for conditions like COPD and asthma, enhancing clinical relevance. Validation on 1,800 subjects demonstrates a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.3 ± 0.4 BPM, root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.7 ± 0.5 BPM, and 94.2% accuracy within ±2 BPM, with 89.7% reliability in high-motion scenarios. Compared to baseline methods (e.g., FFT, Kalman filter), RSP achieves superior performance (p < 0.05). 
 Future work includes real-world trials in rural African clinics and machine learning for automatic comorbidity detection, targeting a 10% MAE reduction. This framework is poised for clinical and consumer health applications, particularly in continuous monitoring for telemedicine.
